criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the automatic sensor-text data generation approach using predefined templates for trend analysis and summary QA pairs.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the Sensor-Language Alignment stage, including the Chronos TS encoder, MLP alignment module, special tokens, and causal LM loss.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the Task-Aware Tuning stage for HAR classification with overlapping windows, multi-channel embedding concatenation, and cross-entropy loss.
The agent has read Section 4.1 and Appendix A.6 to understand the five HAR datasets and their preprocessing configurations including sampling rates, channel counts, splits, and windowing strategies.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the Sensor Data Understanding evaluation results comparing SensorLLM and GPT-4o across all NLP metrics, GPT-4o evaluation, and human evaluation.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the HAR F1-macro and accuracy results for SensorLLM and all 11 baseline models across five datasets.

The agent has read Section 5 and Tables 3, 4, 5 and Figures 3, 4 to understand all ablation studies including alignment removal, textual prompts, MLP depth, model size, special tokens, and cross-dataset generalization.

The agent has read Appendix A.2 to understand the predefined sentence templates for QA generation, including trend description templates (Table 6), question templates (Table 8), and answer templates for summaries (Table 9).

The plan includes automatically generating diverse sensor-text QA pairs from raw sensor data using predefined templates for trend analysis (describing signal changes within a window) and trend summary (summarizing overall behavior).
The plan includes the Sensor-Language Alignment stage using Chronos-large as the frozen TS encoder, LLaMA3-8B as the frozen LLM backbone, and a trainable MLP alignment module.
The plan includes adding two special tokens per sensor channel (e.g., <x_acc_start> and <x_acc_end>) to extend the LLM embedding matrix from V×D to V'×D where V' = V + 2c.
The plan includes the Task-Aware Tuning stage with a trainable linear classifier applied to the LLM's final hidden state to perform HAR classification.

The plan specifies that only the MLP alignment module (5.67% of parameters in Stage 1) and linear classifier (0.12% in Stage 2) are trainable, while the LLM backbone and Chronos encoder remain frozen.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing all five HAR datasets (USC-HAD, UCI-HAR, PAMAP2, MHealth, CAPTURE-24) with dataset-specific sampling rates, channel configurations, subject splits, and windowing strategies.

The plan includes implementing all 11 baseline models across two categories: TS models (Transformer, Informer, NS-Transformer, PatchTST, TimesNet, iTransformer) and HAR models (DeepConvLSTM, DeepConvLSTMAttn, Attend, Chronos+MLP, GPT4TS).

The plan includes Stage 1 training hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-3, 8 epochs, batch size 4, gradient accumulation steps 8, and maximum sequence length 8192 for CAPTURE-24 and 4096 for all other datasets.
The plan includes Stage 2 training with 5 random repetitions to report mean and standard deviation, using 8 epochs, batch size 4, gradient accumulation steps 8, and maximum sequence length 4096.
The plan includes Stage 1 evaluation using NLP metrics (BLEU-1, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-L, METEOR, SBERT, SimCSE), GPT-4o automated evaluation, and human evaluation by five domain experts.

The plan includes all ablation experiments: Task-only (removing alignment), with/without textual prompts, MLP depth comparison (1 vs 2 hidden layers), SensorLLM-3b model size variant, special tokens ablation, and cross-dataset generalization.
The plan includes using GPT-4o as a baseline for Stage 1 evaluation, generating trend descriptions with the prompt template from Table 10 (Appendix A.4).

The code for automatic QA pair generation implements trend analysis templates that describe how sensor signals change within a window using timestamped segment descriptions (e.g., '{start_time}s to {end_time}s: {trend}' from Table 6).

The code for automatic QA pair generation implements all five summary answer template types: trend count, sensor data context, trend change statistics, cumulative trend analysis, and overall trend summary (Table 9).
The code applies instance normalization to each segment before feeding it into Chronos: x_tilde = (x - mean(x_s)) / std(x_s).

The code for Stage 1 implements non-overlapping segmentation where each segment is assigned a random length l within a predefined range, enabling the model to learn from both short-term fluctuations and long-term trends.

The code integrates Chronos-large as the pretrained frozen TS encoder to generate segment embeddings x_hat_s of shape (l+1) x d_ts, where the (l+1) accounts for the EOS token added during Chronos tokenization.

The MLP alignment module is implemented as Equation (1): a_hat_s = W2 * sigma(W1 * x_hat_s + b1) + b2, with W1 in R^(d_m x d_ts) and W2 in R^(D x d_m), mapping sensor embeddings to text-aligned embeddings.
The GELU activation function sigma is used in the MLP alignment module as specified in Section 3.2.
The LLM's embedding matrix is extended from E in R^(V x D) to E in R^(V' x D) where V' = V + 2c, by adding two special token embeddings per sensor channel.

The final channel sensor representation o_hat_s of shape (l+3) x D is constructed by concatenating the channel-specific start special token, aligned sensor embeddings a_hat_s, and channel-specific end special token.
The causal language modeling loss is implemented as Equation (2): L_gen = -sum(log P(z_t^i | Z_t^<i, z_s)), computed only on generated output tokens and not on sensor or instruction input tokens.
The code for Stage 2 implements overlapping windowing with 50% overlap to segment the multivariate sensor data into windows X_S in R^(S x C x L).

The input embedding for the Task-Aware Tuning stage is implemented as Equation (3): z_hat = o_hat_s^1 ⊕ o_hat_s^2 ⊕ ... ⊕ o_hat_s^C ⊕ z_hat_stat, concatenating aligned channel embeddings with statistical features (per-channel mean and variance).

The code extracts the final hidden state h = H_K from the LLM output (leveraging causal masking so the last token encodes all preceding information) to produce a single pooled vector for classification.
A fully connected linear layer projects the pooled LLM hidden state to a prediction vector of size M (number of activity classes), followed by a softmax function to produce class probabilities.
The cross-entropy classification loss is implemented as Equation (4): L_cls = -sum(y_i * log(y_hat_i)), used to optimize the classifier in Stage 2.
The USC-HAD dataset is loaded with 6 channels (3-axis accelerometer and gyroscope) at 100 Hz with 12 activity classes, using subjects 13 and 14 as the test set and remaining subjects for training.
USC-HAD uses a variable window size w in [5, 200] for the alignment stage, and w=200 with stride=100 for the HAR stage.
The UCI-HAR dataset is loaded with 6 channels at 50 Hz with 6 activity classes, using the fixed 70/30 subject-based split provided with the dataset.
UCI-HAR uses a variable window size w in [5, 200] for the alignment stage, and w=128 with stride=64 for the HAR stage.

The PAMAP2 dataset is loaded with 27 channels (acceleration, gyroscope, and magnetometer from chest, hand, and ankle IMUs) downsampled from 100 Hz to 50 Hz, with subjects 105 and 106 as the test set and 12 activity classes.
PAMAP2 uses a variable window size w in [5, 100] for the alignment stage, and w=100 with stride=50 for the HAR stage.

The MHealth dataset is loaded with 15 specific channels (acceleration from chest, left ankle, right lower arm, and gyroscope from left ankle and right lower arm) at 50 Hz, with subjects 1, 3, and 6 as the test set and 12 activity classes.
MHealth uses a variable window size w in [5, 100] for the alignment stage, and w=100 with stride=50 for the HAR stage.

The CAPTURE-24 dataset is loaded with 3-channel wrist-worn accelerometer data, downsampled from 100 Hz to 50 Hz, with the first 100 participants as training and remaining 51 as test, 5% of each subject's data randomly selected, and 10 activity classes.
CAPTURE-24 uses a variable window size w in [10, 500] for the alignment stage, and w=500 with stride=250 for the HAR stage, with maximum sequence length 8192 for Stage 1 training.

The HAR baseline models are implemented for comparison: DeepConvLSTM (4 conv + 2 LSTM layers), DeepConvLSTMAttn (DeepConvLSTM with attention on first 7 hidden states), and Attend (data-agnostic augmentation with intra-class minimization).
The TS baseline models are implemented for comparison: Transformer, Informer, NS-Transformer, PatchTST, TimesNet, and iTransformer.
The Chronos+MLP baseline is implemented by feeding each sensor channel separately into Chronos, concatenating the resulting per-channel embeddings across all channels, and classifying with an MLP.
The GPT4TS baseline is implemented using a frozen pre-trained GPT-2 adapted for time-series classification.
The NLP evaluation metrics BLEU-1, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-L, and METEOR are implemented to evaluate generated trend descriptions against ground-truth text for Stage 1.
The semantic similarity metrics SBERT (cosine similarity of sentence-BERT embeddings) and SimCSE (contrastive sentence embeddings) are implemented to evaluate Stage 1 outputs.
The GPT-4o evaluation pipeline is implemented using the exact prompt from Table 11 (Appendix A.5) to score generated trend descriptions on a 1-5 scale against ground truth.
The F1-macro metric is implemented as defined in Equations (8)-(11): per-class F1 scores averaged equally across all C activity classes, suitable for imbalanced datasets.
The Task-only ablation variant is implemented by skipping the Sensor-Language Alignment stage and directly feeding Chronos embeddings into the LLM for HAR classification.
The two-layer MLP alignment module variant is implemented with architecture 1024→2048→3072→4096 for the MLP depth ablation study.
The SensorLLM-3b variant is implemented using Chronos-base as the TS encoder and LLaMA3.2-3b as the LLM backbone, as a lighter alternative to the full model.
The Stage 1 Sensor-Language Alignment training has been executed successfully on all five datasets (USC-HAD, UCI-HAR, PAMAP2, MHealth, CAPTURE-24) without errors.
The Stage 2 Task-Aware Tuning training for SensorLLM and all 11 baseline models has been executed successfully across all five datasets without errors.
The Stage 1 evaluation pipeline has been executed, sampling 200 instances per dataset for NLP metrics and GPT-4o automated evaluation, and 20 shorter sequences per dataset for human evaluation.
All ablation experiments have been executed successfully: Task-only variant, with/without textual prompts, two-layer MLP on UCI-HAR/PAMAP2/MHealth, and special tokens ablation on PAMAP2 and MHealth.
The cross-dataset generalization experiments have been executed: training Stage 1 on USC-HAD with Stage 2 on UCI-HAR, and training Stage 1 on UCI-HAR with Stage 2 on USC-HAD.
The SensorLLM-3b model size experiments have been executed on USC-HAD, UCI-HAR, and MHealth and compared against the full SensorLLM-8b and the Attend baseline.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: SensorLLM consistently outperforms GPT-4o across all NLP metrics (BLEU-1, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-L, METEOR, SBERT, SimCSE) on all five datasets.
The execution outcomes match the GPT-4o and Human evaluation scores in Table 1: SensorLLM achieves higher GPT-4o and Human scores than GPT-4o-generated descriptions on all five datasets.

The execution outcomes match the main HAR results in Table 2: SensorLLM achieves best F1-macro on four out of five datasets (USC-HAD: 61.2%, PAMAP2: 86.2%, MHealth: 89.4%, CAPTURE-24: 48.6%) and second best on UCI-HAR (91.2%).

The execution outcomes match Table 2: SensorLLM substantially outperforms Chronos+MLP on all datasets (e.g., CAPTURE-24: 48.6% vs 38.0%, MHealth: 89.4% vs 83.0%), validating that the alignment stage provides gains beyond the Chronos encoder alone.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: SensorLLM achieves a +5.0% absolute F1-macro gain over Attend on CAPTURE-24 (48.6% vs 43.6%) and +6.0% on MHealth (89.4% vs 83.4%).

The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: SensorLLM consistently outperforms the Task-only variant across all five datasets both with and without textual prompts, confirming the necessity of the Sensor-Language Alignment stage.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: incorporating textual prompts (statistical features for each sensor channel) consistently improves F1-macro scores across all datasets for both Task-only and SensorLLM variants.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: adding special tokens improves F1-macro on PAMAP2 (84.4 without ST vs 85.8 with ST) and MHealth (89.6 without ST vs 90.2 with ST) with a two-layer MLP.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: SensorLLM trained with Stage 1 on a different dataset achieves comparable performance to same-dataset alignment (USC-HAD Stage 2 with UCI-HAR Stage 1: 61.6% vs baseline 61.2%, UCI-HAR Stage 2 with USC-HAD Stage 1: 91.0% vs baseline 91.2%).

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: using a two-layer MLP (1024→2048→3072→4096) shows mixed results—improving F1-macro on UCI-HAR (91.2→92.0%) and MHealth (89.4→90.2%) but slightly decreasing on PAMAP2 (86.2→85.8%).

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: SensorLLM-3b outperforms Attend on USC-HAD (60.4% vs 60.2%) and MHealth (88.4% vs 83.4%) while slightly trailing on UCI-HAR (90.8% vs 93.2%), confirming the 3b model provides a strong efficiency-performance trade-off.
